title: Sub-second Latency AND 4K Video Quality
problem_statement:
A cloud gaming platform streams real-time game video to players over the internet. Competitive gaming requires controller-to-photon latency below 50ms to maintain playability, while the visual quality must meet 4K HDR standards (3840x2160, 10-bit color depth, 60fps minimum) to justify the premium subscription price.

Uncompressed 4K HDR at 60fps requires 12 Gbps bandwidth. Even with H.265/HEVC encoding, maintaining visual quality above 95 VMAF requires a minimum 35 Mbps bitrate, but 38% of target subscribers have connections below 25 Mbps. Aggressive compression to 15 Mbps (reachable by 89% of users) drops VMAF to 78, producing visible artifacts in fast-motion scenes.

Additionally, the encode-transmit-decode pipeline adds 42ms of latency at the 35 Mbps quality level, consuming 84% of the total latency budget before accounting for network transit (8-35ms) and display pipeline (8ms).

The system must deliver both pristine visual quality and imperceptible latency over typical consumer internet connections.

ground_truth.ifr_baseline: Players see perfect 4K quality with zero perceivable latency over any internet connection
